### Problem ###
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report on the revised `answer.tex`

### Verdict

The revision is more honest and clearer than the previous draft, but it is **not a complete solution**. The document explicitly leaves two essential local-to-global inputs unresolved, states only a **conditional** order-reversal theorem, and ends with “Remaining open issues.” Under the requested standard, this is automatically **not answer-ready**.

I also found a few additional issues in the conditional proof, especially missing edge-case handling in Theorem 3.2.

---

## Literature and external validation

The use of Brandt–Eur–Zhang for the valuated-quotient / tropical-linear-space inclusion dictionary is appropriate: BEZ state that \(\mu\preceq\nu\) iff \(\operatorname{trop}(\mu)\subseteq\operatorname{trop}(\nu)\), and they identify the full arbitrary incidence-Plücker relations with valuated matroid quotients. ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))

The revision correctly retreats from the previous invalid use of Murota–Shioura on a union of two non-adjacent basis layers. Murota–Shioura’s local theorem requires an \(M^\natural\)-convex effective domain and a local exchange property, so it does not automatically imply the desired nested-to-full incidence criterion for two arbitrary non-adjacent ranks. ([educ.titech.ac.jp](https://educ.titech.ac.jp/iee/publications/file/pub_15646.pdf))

Jarra–Lorscheid remains relevant background for flag matroids over tracts and cryptomorphic descriptions, but the revised draft correctly recognizes that a precise nonuniform \(\infty\)-valued adjacent-local criterion still has to be supplied before Proposition 2.4 becomes unconditional. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S000187082300539X))

---

## LaTeX contract check

I compiled the revised `answer.tex` twice with `pdflatex`. It compiled successfully and produced a 5-page PDF. The document class is exactly

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
```

It uses `fullpage`, which is permitted, and I found no prohibited margin/layout, line-spacing, or in-document font-size changes. So I found **no LaTeX-contract violation**.

---

## What the revision fixed from the previous review

1. **Invalid Murota–Shioura shortcut removed.**  
   The previous draft asserted that the union of two non-adjacent basis layers was a generalized polymatroid / \(M^\natural\)-convex domain. The revision now explicitly acknowledges this was invalid and isolates the nested-to-full incidence implication as an unresolved Criterion.

2. **Circuit-minimum lemma improved.**  
   The revised proof now chooses one complement \(D\) to the flat \(F=\operatorname{cl}(A\cup C)\), fixing the ambiguity in the previous version.

3. **Ordinary polar-rank argument strengthened.**  
   The revised Lemma 2.1 gives a more explicit monotonicity/unit-increment/submodularity proof. It is much better than before, though still somewhat compressed.

4. **Open issues are now explicitly acknowledged.**  
   This is good scholarly practice, but it means the document cannot count as a final solution.

---

## Detailed audit

### Section 0: Conventions and local inputs

The valuated-matroid model of \(\Dr(M)\) is reasonable and supported by BEZ. The answer now says explicitly that it is using this standard model. That addresses part of my previous concern about the identification between tropical linear subspaces and projective valuated matroids.

However, the central **Criterion 0.1** is unproved:

\[
\text{nested incidence }(N)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\text{full arbitrary incidence }(A).
\]

This is not a minor lemma. It is essential to the proof of Theorem 3.2. The draft says this is “the precise remaining place where a proof or reference is still needed.” Therefore the main theorem is conditional.

The one-point completion formula (1) is also still supported only by a broad reference to Murota / Dress–Terhalle. This may be standard, but a final proof should cite a precise theorem or give a self-contained verification that all \(h_j\) are valuated matroids and that \(h_j\preceq h_{j+1}\). The endpoint \(h_r\simeq\mu_M\) is checked, but the internal layers are not proved in the text.

The complete-flag local criterion is also explicitly left open. Since Proposition 2.4 depends on it, Proposition 2.4 is not an unconditional result.

### Section 1: Flat-lattice duality and flat constancy

Lemma 1.1 is essentially correct. A rank-reversing lattice anti-automorphism of the flat lattice forces modularity by applying the anti-automorphism to semimodularity.

Lemma 1.2 is also substantially correct. The revised proof now states the common-complement argument more clearly. The incidence relation with \(\mu_M\) forces equality of values on independent \(k\)-sets spanning the same \(M\)-flat. This part is acceptable.

### Section 2: Polar transform and supports

The definition of

\[
\nu^{\perp_M}(A)=\bar\nu((\operatorname{cl}A)^\perp)
\]

for \(M\)-independent \(A\) of size \(r-k\) is well-defined using flat constancy, and involutivity on flat coordinates is plausible.

Lemma 2.1, the ordinary polar-rank lemma, is much improved. The formula

\[
\rho^\perp(A)=\operatorname{rk}_M(A)-k+\rho_N((\operatorname{cl}A)^\perp)
\]

does plausibly define a matroid rank function. The proof should still explicitly note that \(\rho_N\) is constant on \(M\)-closures because \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\); this is used in the submodularity and closure-extension argument. But this is probably fixable and not the main fatal issue.

Lemma 2.2, the elementary opposition lemma, is plausible. Some details remain terse, especially in the \(\delta=2\) degeneration cases, but I do not see a clear fatal error there.

Proposition 2.4 is explicitly conditional on the complete-flag local criterion. Since that criterion is not proved or precisely cited, Proposition 2.4 does not establish that \(\nu^{\perp_M}\) is always a point of \(\Dr(M)\). This is one of the two main unresolved gaps.

### Section 3: Conditional order reversal

The circuit-minimum lemma is now acceptable.

The nested-incidence case analysis in Theorem 3.2 remains plausible, but the theorem is only conditional on Proposition 2.4 and Criterion 0.1. Thus it does not prove the required order reversal in \(\Dr(M)\).

There are also some **new or reintroduced edge-case issues**:

1. **Case \(b=r\).**  
   The proof starts by fixing \(A\subseteq T\) with \(|A|=r-b-1\). If \(b=r\), this gives \(|A|=-1\), so the proof as written is invalid. The previous draft had a separate edge-case paragraph; the revised version removed it.

2. **Case \(a=0\).**  
   The \(\delta=m\) case would involve a flat \(G\) of rank \(a-1=-1\), although that case may be impossible by rank bounds. This should be stated explicitly.

3. **Case \(a=b\).**  
   Criterion 0.1 is stated only for \(a<b\). Theorem 3.2 states \(p\preceq q\) without excluding equal ranks. Equal-rank quotient comparisons may be trivial under suitable conventions, but the proof does not say so. A final proof needs a separate equal-rank argument.

These edge cases are not the main reason for non-readiness, but they must be fixed in any complete version.

### Section 4: Agreement with flats

This section is essentially correct, conditional on the polar transform being well-defined on all of \(\Dr(M)\). The modular-rank argument

\[
Y^\perp\vee F=\hat 1
\iff
Y^\perp\wedge F=\hat 0
\iff
Y\vee F^\perp=\hat 1
\]

is sound.

### Remaining open issues section

This section accurately identifies the two fatal missing ingredients:

1. nested incidence \(\Rightarrow\) full arbitrary incidence for two ranks;
2. complete-flag adjacent-local criterion / direct proof that each polar layer is valuated.

Because these are essential, the document itself acknowledges that the proof is incomplete.

---

## Additional code check

Besides compiling the LaTeX, I ran a small sanity check for the nested-versus-full incidence issue in the uniform \(a=2,b=4,n=6\), \(\{0,1\}\)-valued setting. I found no counterexample in that small search, but this does not prove Criterion 0.1 and does not repair the proof.

---

## Final assessment

The revision successfully turns the previous overclaimed proof into a transparent **conditional argument** with the main gaps isolated. That is an improvement, but it is not a complete rigorous solution to the stated problem. In particular, `answer.tex` still contains unproved essential criteria and explicitly lists remaining open issues.

### Council member: gpt-55-pro (models/openai/gpt-55-pro) ###
I think this criterion is true, and the clean proof is not via Murota--Shioura on the union of the two layers, but via the vector-generation theorem for a single valuated matroid.

Here is the route I would try to write rigorously.

For \(|K|=a-1\), write
\[
v_K^p(j)=
\begin{cases}
p(Kj),& j\notin K,\\
\infty,& j\in K,
\end{cases}
\]
viewed as a valuated “fundamental vector/cocircuit” of \(p\). Fix an arbitrary upper set \(J\in\binom E{b+1}\), and put
\[
y_J(j)=q(J-j)\qquad (j\in J).
\]
If all \(y_J(j)=\infty\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise some \(J-j\) is a \(q\)-basis, and since the ordinary supports are in quotient order, \(J\) spans the support matroid of \(p\). Thus the restriction \(p|J\) has rank \(a\).

Now the nested hypotheses with \(T=J\) say exactly that
\[
\min_{j\in J\setminus S}\{p(Sj)+y_J(j)\}
\]
is attained twice for every \(S\in\binom Ja-1\). Equivalently, \(y_J\) is tropically orthogonal to every fundamental vector \(v_S^{p|J}\) of the restricted valuated matroid \(p|J\).

Use the standard theorem: the vector set of a valuated matroid is tropically generated by its fundamental vectors/circuits; equivalently, for a spanning restriction \(p|J\), every restricted vector \(v_I^p|_J\) lies in the tropical span of the \(v_S^p|_J\) with \(S\subseteq J\), \(|S|=a-1\). This is the usual valuated circuit-elimination/generation statement; I would cite Dress--Wenzel or Murota’s DCA treatment of valuated matroid vectors, or Speyer’s “tropical linear spaces” discussion that valuated circuits generate the tropical linear space.

Finally, for fixed \(y_J\), the set of vectors tropically orthogonal to \(y_J\) is a tropical hyperplane, hence tropically convex. Since it contains all generators \(v_S^{p|J}\), it contains \(v_I^p|_J\) for every arbitrary \(I\in\binom E{a-1}\). Written out, this says
\[
\min_{j\in J\setminus I}\{p(Ij)+q(J-j)\}
\]
is attained at least twice, which is exactly the full BEZ incidence relation for this \(I,J\).

So the key lemma to isolate is:

> If \(p\) is rank \(a\), \(J\) spans \(p\), and \(I\in\binom E{a-1}\), then \(j\mapsto p(Ij)\) restricted to \(J\) is in the tropical span of the fundamental vectors \(j\mapsto p(Sj)\) with \(S\subseteq J\), \(|S|=a-1\).

That lemma is standard valuated-matroid vector generation, including the nonuniform/\(\infty\)-valued case via elimination. This should give a short rigorous proof of the two-rank local quotient criterion.

### Council member: opus_47_max (models/anthropic/opus_47_max) ###
I think the criterion is plausibly true (your Z3 evidence + the classical ordinary-matroid analog), but I don't know a clean off-the-shelf reference. Three angles plus a pragmatic suggestion:

**1. $M^\natural$-concavity on the *flag-basis* polytope (not the union of layers).** Encode pairs $(B_a, B_b)$ with $B_a \subseteq B_b$ as $x \in \{0,1,2\}^E$ ($x_e = 0/1/2$ for $e \notin B_b / e \in B_b\setminus B_a / e \in B_a$), with $\sum[x\ge 1]=b$, $\sum[x=2]=a$. This is a *single* generalized polymatroid, and $F(x) := p(B_a)+q(B_b)$ is $M^\natural$-concave on it iff three-term Plücker for $p,q$ plus your nested incidence hold. Murota–Shioura 2018 promotes to global $M^\natural$-concavity.

*Snag.* The lattice points of this polytope are flag pairs, so global $M^\natural$ gives arbitrary BEZ only when $I\subseteq J$. For non-nested $I,J$ the pairs $(I+i,J-i)$ have $\delta\in I\setminus J$ with $\delta\in I+i\setminus(J-i)$, so they lie outside the polytope. This route does *not* close the criterion as stated.

**2. Murota multi-step exchange** ("Convexity and Steinitz's exchange property," Adv. Math. 1996; DCA Ch. 5). The closest neighborhood is valuated matroid intersection / valuated bimatroid. I don't know a clean statement covering cross-rank BEZ at non-adjacent ranks — would require a careful read.

**3. Direct bypass: prove arbitrary polar BEZ.** Section 3 uses Criterion 1 only to upgrade *nested* polar BEZ to *arbitrary* polar BEZ. Try the latter directly using arbitrary BEZ for $(p,q)$ (your hypothesis). Set $A=I\cap J$, $B=J\setminus I$, $C=I\setminus J$, $c=|C|$.

*Snag here too.* Generically $\rk\cl(I\cup J)-\rk\cl(A)=b-a+2+2c$, so the polar dual interval has $|B|+|C|$ atoms while you sum over only $|B|=b-a+2+c$ of them. A non-nested polar BEZ pair $(I,J)$ does not dualize to a single original BEZ pair $(I',J')$ of the standard sizes $(a-1,b+1)$ — it dualizes to an enlarged dual. You'd need either (i) a Boolean superposition of multiple original BEZ relations or (ii) verification that dependencies introduced by $C$ collapse the non-nested polar relations to triviality (all $\infty$). I'd recommend writing out $c=1$ explicitly first; it should tell you which.

**Counterexample search.** Smallest meaningful test for the criterion itself is $a=1$, $b=3$, $|E|=5$ — small enough for direct enumeration over $\{0,1,\ldots,N\}\cup\{\infty\}$ beyond just SAT UNSAT.

**Pragmatic recommendation.** In your next round, work out the $c=1$ case for Path 3. The structure should either (a) yield arbitrary polar BEZ for free (best outcome), (b) expose a structural obstruction pointing to Path 2, or (c) produce a near-counterexample suggesting Path 2/Path 1 fails. This is the smallest test that distinguishes the three routes.

My prior is "probably true, with proof requiring genuine combinatorics" — the fact that no obvious off-the-shelf theorem applies despite the criterion's natural form is itself a warning. If you do prove it, the cleanest organization is likely as a lemma about $M^\natural$-concave functions on the flag $\{0,1,2\}^E$ polytope plus a *separate* argument bridging nested-flag-pair BEZ to non-flag-pair BEZ — and that second bridge is where the real work is.

Uncertain about Path 2's exact applicability; medium-confident on the $c=1$ test being diagnostic.

### Council member: gemini-31-pro (models/gemini/gemini-31-pro) ###
The two-rank local quotient criterion is rigorously **true**. You do not need to rely on $M^\natural$-convexity of the non-adjacent union, nor do you need to construct a counterexample. 

For an exact literature reference, this is explicitly **Proposition 3.5 in Brandt--Eur--Zhang (2021)** (*Tropical flag varieties*). They prove that for any valuated matroids $p, q$, the full quotient relation $p \preceq q$ is equivalent to checking the nested incidence relations $S \subset T$. 

If you prefer to keep your paper fully self-contained, you can bypass the heavy machinery of multiple exchange and prove it directly using a beautiful, short induction on the distance $k = |I \setminus J|$. Here is the exact induction you need:

**Proof sketch:**
We want to show that $V(v) := p(I \cup v) + q(J \setminus v)$ attains its minimum over $v \in J \setminus I$ at least twice. We are given the base case $k = 0$ (which is exactly your nested incidence criterion $I \subset J$).

Assume $k > 0$ and the claim holds for $k-1$. Suppose for contradiction that $V(v)$ has a unique strict minimum at $i_0 \in J \setminus I$. 
Pick $x \in I \setminus J$. For any $u, v \in J \setminus I$, define the symmetric matrix:
\[ F(u, v) = p((I \setminus x) \cup \{u, v\}) + q(J \setminus u) + q(J \setminus v) \]
For any fixed $u \in J \setminus I$, let $I_u = (I \setminus x) \cup \{u\}$. Note that $|I_u \setminus J| = k-1$. By the induction hypothesis for $I_u$ and $J$, the sequence $p(I_u \cup v) + q(J \setminus v)$ attains its minimum twice over $v$. Adding the constant $q(J \setminus u)$, this means **every row $u$ of $F(u, v)$ attains its minimum at least twice**.

1. Let $y$ be an element minimizing row $i_0$, so $F(i_0, y) = \min_v F(i_0, v) := M$.
2. Look at row $y$. One of its entries is $F(y, i_0) = F(i_0, y) = M$. Because row $y$ must attain its minimum twice, there is some $w \neq i_0$ such that $F(y, w) \le M$.
3. Apply the 3-term valuated Plücker relation for $p$ on the sets $X = I$ (size $a-1$) and $Y = (I \setminus x) \cup \{i_0, y, w\}$ (size $a+1$). The minimum of the following three terms must be attained twice:
   - $A = p(I \cup i_0) + p((I \setminus x) \cup \{y, w\})$
   - $B = p(I \cup y) + p((I \setminus x) \cup \{i_0, w\})$
   - $C = p(I \cup w) + p((I \setminus x) \cup \{i_0, y\})$
4. Shift all three terms by adding $q(J \setminus i_0) + q(J \setminus y) + q(J \setminus w)$. The terms magically group into:
   - $\tilde{A} = V(i_0) + F(y, w)$
   - $\tilde{B} = V(y) + F(i_0, w)$
   - $\tilde{C} = V(w) + F(i_0, y) = V(w) + M$

By our contradiction assumption, $i_0$ uniquely minimizes $V$, so $V(i_0) < V(y)$ and $V(i_0) < V(w)$. 
- Since $F(y, w) \le M$, we have $\tilde{A} \le V(i_0) + M$.
- Since $y$ minimizes row $i_0$, $F(i_0, w) \ge M$, so $\tilde{B} \ge V(y) + M > V(i_0) + M \ge \tilde{A}$.
- Finally, $\tilde{C} = V(w) + M > V(i_0) + M \ge \tilde{A}$.

Thus $\tilde{A}$ is strictly smaller than both $\tilde{B}$ and $\tilde{C}$. This means $\min(\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}, \tilde{C})$ is attained only once, flatly contradicting the Plücker relation for $p$. 

This directly bridges your nested bounds to the arbitrary incidence relations without relying on undefined generalized polymatroid domains.

# Round 7 report

I read the current `answer.tex` and `research_notes.tex`.  The two genuinely open items are exactly the ones isolated there.  My conclusion is:

* The complete-flag adjacent criterion has a clean nonuniform, \(\infty\)-valued reference/proof via Jidong Wang's codimension-one relative Dressian theorem.
* I did not find a proof or exact theorem for the two-rank non-adjacent endpoint criterion.  I ran targeted Z3 searches; no small counterexample was found in the requested rank \((2,4)\) cases, but the theorem remains unproved from this round.

## 1. Complete-flag adjacent criterion: resolved

The relevant reference is:

> Jidong Wang, *Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces*, arXiv:2412.12059.  In the local source `papers/wang_2412.12059_src/arXiv2026.tex`, see Theorem `\ref{thm:dressian-cut-out-by-three-term}`: “Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid. Then \(\Dr^1(\mu)\) is cut out by the three-term incidence relations.”  Around line 420 Wang explicitly says this extends Joswig--Loho--Luber--Olarte Theorem 3.10, which treated the uniform finite case.

Wang proves the nonuniform support issues directly.  In the same source, the preceding support lemma proves that a rank \(d-1\) function \(\theta\) satisfying the incidence relations against a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(\mu\) has matroidal support; the theorem then proves all incidence relations from the three-term ones.  The proof allows \(\infty\)-values and handles degenerate/parallel hyperplanes separately before the concurrent three-term case.

LaTeX-ready replacement statement:

```tex
\begin{proposition}[complete-flag adjacent criterion]
Let \(w_0,\ldots,w_r\) be functions
\[
        w_j:\binom Ej\longrightarrow \mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
with nonempty ordinary complete-flag support, with \(w_0(\emptyset)=0\),
and with \(w_r\) a valuated matroid.  Suppose that for every
\(1\le j<r\), every \(S\in\binom E{j-1}\), and every distinct
\(x,y,z\notin S\), the adjacent tropical incidence relation
\[
\min\{w_j(Sx)+w_{j+1}(Syz),\,
      w_j(Sy)+w_{j+1}(Sxz),\,
      w_j(Sz)+w_{j+1}(Sxy)\}
\]
is attained at least twice, with the usual all-\(\infty\) convention.
Then each \(w_j\) is a valuated matroid and
\[
        w_0\preceq w_1\preceq\cdots\preceq w_r .
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Descend on \(j\).  Assume \(w_{j+1}\) is a valuated matroid.
Wang's theorem that the codimension-one relative Dressian
\(\Dr^1(w_{j+1})\) is cut out by the three-term incidence relations
applies without a uniformity hypothesis and with \(\infty\)-valued
coordinates.  The displayed adjacent relations are precisely those
three-term incidence relations for a rank \(j\) elementary quotient of
\(w_{j+1}\).  Hence \(w_j\) is a rank \(j\) valuated matroid and
\(w_j\preceq w_{j+1}\).  Starting from the valuated top layer \(w_r\)
and iterating proves this for \(j=r-1,\ldots,1\).  The rank-zero layer
is the trivial valuated matroid and is automatically a quotient of
\(w_1\).  Composition of valuated quotients is
Brandt--Eur--Zhang Corollary 4.3.2, equivalently Jarra--Lorscheid
Theorem 2.17/Proposition 2.21 over the perfect tropical tract.
\end{proof}
```

This is stronger and more precise than citing Murota--Shioura local
\(M^\natural\)-exchange.  Murota--Shioura Theorem 4.2 requires the full
local \(M^\natural\)-exchange package \(L1,L2,L3\) on an
\(M^\natural\)-convex domain; the displayed adjacent flag equations are
only the cross-rank \(L2\)-type equations.  Wang's theorem is exactly the
missing nonuniform adjacent-rank Pluecker-generation result.

Related references:

* Joswig--Loho--Luber--Olarte, *Generalized permutahedra and positive flag Dressians*, IMRN 2023, Theorem 3.10: finite uniform adjacent incidence implies the two adjacent functions are valuated matroids.
* Brandt--Eur--Zhang, *Tropical flag varieties*, Adv. Math. 384 (2021), Definition 4.2.1, Proposition 4.2.3, Theorem 4.3.1, Corollary 4.3.2: incidence-Pluecker relations, valuated flag matroids, tropical linear-space inclusions, and composition of valuated quotients.
* Jarra--Lorscheid, *Flag matroids with coefficients*, Adv. Math. 436 (2024), Theorem 2.17 and Proposition 2.21: over the perfect tropical tract, adjacent quotient chains are flags, but note this assumes the individual layers are already valuated matroids; Wang supplies that missing adjacent Pluecker-generation step.

## 2. Two-rank local quotient criterion

I did not find an exact theorem proving the desired non-adjacent criterion:

\[
\text{individual valuated matroids}
 + \text{ordinary support quotient}
 + \text{nested endpoint incidence }(S\subseteq T)
 \Longrightarrow \text{full BEZ incidence.}
\]

The closest literature I found is useful but not sufficient:

* Brandenburg--Loho--Smith, *Quotients of M-convex sets and M-convex functions*, arXiv:2403.07751, Theorem 1.3 and Remark 5.7.  Their exchange condition (C) is the full BEZ-valuated quotient exchange for arbitrary pairs of bases, and in the valuated matroid case it is equivalent to the minimizer/initial-matroid quotient condition.  However, the nested endpoint equations are only the special case \(|B_p\setminus B_q|=1\) of that exchange condition.  I found no statement in BLS that this restricted local case promotes to full exchange for non-elementary rank gaps.
* Murota--Shioura, *Simpler exchange axioms for M-concave functions on generalized polymatroids*, Theorem 4.2, is not directly applicable: the union of two non-adjacent homogeneous layers is generally not an \(M^\natural\)-convex domain, e.g. the union of the rank \(2\) and rank \(4\) layers of \(U_{4,6}\).
* Murota's multiple exchange theorem still looks like the right possible tool for an induction on \(|B_p\setminus B_q|\), but I did not manage to close the cancellation/monotonicity step.

So I recommend leaving Criterion `\ref{crit:local}` open unless the Author can supply an induction proof.  A safe wording is: “This is not a consequence of the standard local \(M^\natural\)-exchange theorem, since the two-layer domain need not be \(M^\natural\)-convex.”

## 3. Z3 searches for the two-rank criterion

I wrote `code/two_rank_local_criterion_z3.py`.  It encodes:

* rank \(a,b\) variables over \(\{0,\ldots,K,\infty\}\);
* individual tropical Pluecker relations for \(p\) and \(q\);
* ordinary support quotient order using the \(0/\infty\) full incidence condition;
* all nested endpoint incidence relations for values;
* a requested unique-min failure of a non-nested full incidence relation.

For rank \((2,4)\), a failed non-nested relation is, up to relabeling, \(I=\{0\}\), \(J=\{1,2,3,4,5\}\) on \(n=6\), and likewise with one unused element on \(n=7\).  Thus the fixed-relation UNSAT runs are WLOG, not merely partial checks.

| case | alphabet | result | log |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| \(n=6,(a,b)=(2,4)\) | \(\{0,1\}\) finite | UNSAT | `data/two_rank_local_n6_a2_b4_K1_finite.json` |
| \(n=6,(2,4)\) | \(\{0,1,\infty\}\) | UNSAT | `data/two_rank_local_n6_a2_b4_K1_inf.json` |
| \(n=6,(2,4)\), fixed failure WLOG | \(\{0,1,2\}\) finite | UNSAT | `data/two_rank_local_n6_a2_b4_K2_finite_fixed.json` |
| \(n=6,(2,4)\), fixed failure WLOG | \(\{0,1,2,\infty\}\) | UNSAT | `data/two_rank_local_n6_a2_b4_K2_inf_fixed.json` |
| \(n=7,(2,4)\), fixed failure WLOG | \(\{0,1,\infty\}\) | UNSAT | `data/two_rank_local_n7_a2_b4_K1_inf_fixed.json` |
| \(n=7,(2,4)\), fixed failure WLOG | \(\{0,1,2,\infty\}\) | timeout/unknown | `data/two_rank_local_n7_a2_b4_K2_inf_fixed.json` |

No counterexample was found.  The \(n=7,K=2,\infty\) case timed out after 180 seconds.

## 4. Cautionary support check

I also patched `code/complete_flag_adjacent_search.py` to optionally enforce ordinary support-matroid constraints.  Before adding such constraints, the Boolean \(U_{6,6}\) complete-flag search found an invalid “counterexample” with rank-3 finite support
\[
\{024,\ 045,\ 134,\ 245\},
\]
all values equal.  It fails the rank-3 Pluecker relation with \(S=\{4\}\) and \(\{0,1,2,3\}\): only the term \((024,134)\) is finite.  This confirms that the ordinary support hypothesis in the complete-flag criterion is essential, but it is not a counterexample to the criterion as stated.

from pathlib import Path
answer = r"""\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newcommand{\cL}{\mathcal L}
\newcommand{\Dr}{\operatorname{Dr}}
\newcommand{\Trop}{\operatorname{Trop}}
\newcommand{\rk}{\operatorname{rk}}
\newcommand{\cl}{\operatorname{cl}}
\newcommand{\op}{\operatorname{op}}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}
\newtheorem{criterion}{Criterion}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf A polar transform for the relative Dressian}
\end{center}

\section*{0. Conventions and two local facts}

Let $E$ be the ground set, $r=\rk M$, and $\mu_M$ the trivial rank $r$
valuated matroid supported on the bases of $M$.  I use the standard
model of the relative Dressian: a rank $k$ point is a projective
valuated matroid $\nu$ with $\nu\preceq\mu_M$, where $\preceq$ denotes
valuated quotient.  Brandt--Eur--Zhang identify this order with
inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces,
\[
        \Trop(\theta)\subseteq\Trop(\nu)
        \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \theta\preceq\nu .
\]
For valuated matroids $p,q$ of ranks $a\le b$, $p\preceq q$ is
equivalent to the incidence-Plucker relations
\[
 \min_{i\in J\setminus I}\{p(I\cup i)+q(J\setminus i)\}
 \quad\hbox{attained at least twice}                         \tag{IP}
\]
for all $I\in\binom{E}{a-1}$ and $J\in\binom{E}{b+1}$.  All-$\infty$
minima are regarded as attained at every displayed index.

\begin{criterion}[nested incidence suffices]\label{crit:local}
Let $p,q$ be valuated matroids of ranks $a<b$ whose ordinary supports
are in quotient order.  If {\rm (IP)} holds whenever $I\subseteq J$,
then {\rm (IP)} holds for all $I,J$.
\end{criterion}

\begin{proof}
For a rank $a$ valuated matroid $p$ and an $(a-1)$-set $I$, write
$C^*_p(I)_e=p(I\cup e)$, with value $\infty$ if $e\in I$.  We use the
standard cocircuit-generation theorem of Dress--Wenzel/Speyer: the
tropical linear space of a valuated matroid is the tropical convex hull
of its valuated cocircuits.

Fix arbitrary $I\in\binom{E}{a-1}$ and $J\in\binom{E}{b+1}$, and put
$c_j=q(J\setminus j)$ for $j\in J$.  If all $c_j$ are $\infty$ there is
nothing to prove.  Otherwise $J$ contains a $q$-basis.  Since the
ordinary support of $p$ is a quotient of the ordinary support of $q$,
every $q$-basis spans the support matroid of $p$; hence the restriction
$p|J$ has rank $a$.

For every $S\subseteq J$ with $|S|=a-1$, the nested hypothesis with
upper set $J$ says precisely that the cocircuit $C^*_{p|J}(S)$ is
tropically orthogonal to $c=(c_j)_{j\in J}$.  The set of vectors
orthogonal to a fixed $c$ is a tropical hyperplane, hence is tropically
convex; therefore it contains the whole tropical linear space of $p|J$.
It remains only to note that $C^*_p(I)|_J$ is a vector of $p|J$: for
each valuated circuit of $p|J$, indexed by a set
$D\in\binom{J}{a+1}$, the orthogonality relation
\[
 \min_{e\in D}\{p(I\cup e)+p(D\setminus e)\}
\]
is exactly a Plucker relation for $p$.  Hence $C^*_p(I)|_J$ is
orthogonal to $c$, which is the desired arbitrary incidence relation.
\end{proof}

We also use one standard codimension-one local theorem.  Wang proved
that, for any valuated matroid $\eta$, the codimension-one relative
Dressian $\Dr^1(\eta)$ is cut out by the three-term incidence
relations.  Consequently:

\begin{proposition}[complete adjacent flags]\label{prop:flagcriterion}
Let $w_0,\ldots,w_r$ be nonzero functions
$w_j:\binom{E}{j}\to{\mathbb R}\cup\{\infty\}$, with $w_0$ the trivial
rank $0$ valuation and $w_r$ a valuated matroid.  Suppose that for each
$1\le j<r$, each $S\in\binom{E}{j-1}$, and distinct $x,y,z\notin S$,
\[
\min\{w_j(Sx)+w_{j+1}(Syz),\,
      w_j(Sy)+w_{j+1}(Sxz),\,
      w_j(Sz)+w_{j+1}(Sxy)\}
\]
is attained at least twice.  Then every $w_j$ is a valuated matroid and
\[
        w_0\preceq w_1\preceq\cdots\preceq w_r .
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Descend on $j$.  If $w_{j+1}$ is valuated, Wang's theorem says exactly
that the displayed three-term relations cut out the rank $j$ elementary
quotients of $w_{j+1}$.  Thus $w_j$ is valuated and
$w_j\preceq w_{j+1}$.  Iteration from $w_r$ proves the assertion; the
rank-zero layer is the unique rank-zero valuated matroid.
\end{proof}

Finally, if $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ has rank $k$, we use its valuated Higgs
completion
\[
 h_j(B)=
 \begin{cases}
 \min\{\nu(C): B\subseteq C,\ |C|=k\}, & j\le k,\ |B|=j,\\
 \min\{\nu(C): C\subseteq B,\ |C|=k\}, & j\ge k,\ B\in\mathcal I_j(M),\\
 \infty, & j\ge k,\ B\notin\mathcal I_j(M).
 \end{cases}                                                \tag{1}
\]
Murota's truncation and relative elongation theorems, equivalently the
Dress--Terhalle well-layering construction, give valuated matroids
$h_j$ and adjacent quotients $h_j\preceq h_{j+1}$.  The top layer is
projectively $\mu_M$: if $B'=B-e+f$ are adjacent $M$-bases and
$C\subseteq B$ minimizes (1), then either $e\notin C$ and $C\subseteq B'$,
or the incidence relation for $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $C-e$ and
upper set $B\cup f$ gives a $k$-subset of $B'$ of value at most
$\nu(C)$; by symmetry the two top-layer values are equal.

\section*{1. Flat-lattice duality and flat constancy}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:modular}
If $\cL(M)$ admits an order-reversing involution $X\mapsto X^\perp$,
then $\cL(M)$ is modular and $\rk(X^\perp)=r-\rk(X)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The anti-automorphism reverses maximal chains, so it sends rank $i$ to
rank $r-i$.  Applying it to the semimodular inequality for the geometric
lattice $\cL(M)$ gives the reverse inequality, hence equality.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[flat constancy]\label{lem:flatconst}
Let $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ have rank $k$.  If $B,B'$ are independent
$k$-subsets of $M$ with $\cl(B)=\cl(B')$, then $\nu(B)=\nu(B')$; and
$\nu(A)=\infty$ whenever $A$ is dependent in $M$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The support matroid of $\nu$ is an ordinary quotient of $M$, so its
bases are independent in $M$.  For $k>0$ it suffices to compare
$B=A\cup b$ and $B'=A\cup b'$ spanning the same rank $k$ flat.  Choose
one complement $C$ so that both $AbC$ and $Ab'C$ are $M$-bases.  In the
incidence relation for $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $A$ and upper
set $A\cup\{b,b'\}\cup C$, the only finite $\mu_M$-terms are the
$b$- and $b'$-terms.  Thus $\nu(Ab)=\nu(Ab')$.
\end{proof}

Write $\bar\nu(X)$ for the common value on independent $k$-sets spanning
the rank $k$ flat $X$, and put $\bar\nu(X)=\infty$ if no basis of $\nu$
spans $X$.

\section*{2. The polar transform}

For a rank $k$ quotient $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ define
\[
 \nu^{\perp_M}(A)=
 \begin{cases}
   \bar\nu\bigl((\cl A)^\perp\bigr),
      & A\text{ independent in }M,\ |A|=r-k,\\
   \infty,&\text{otherwise.}
 \end{cases}                                                 \tag{2}
\]
This is projectively well-defined, and applying it twice recovers the
original flat coordinates.

\begin{lemma}[ordinary polar rank]\label{lem:ordinary}
Let $N$ be an ordinary quotient of $M$ of rank $k$, with rank function
$\rho_N$.  Then
\[
        \rho^\perp(A)=\rk_M(A)-k+\rho_N((\cl A)^\perp)          \tag{3}
\]
is a matroid rank function.  Its bases are precisely the finite support
of (2).  If $N_p$ is a quotient of $N_q$, then
$N_q^{\perp_M}$ is a quotient of $N_p^{\perp_M}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Since $N$ is a quotient of $M$, $\rho_N$ is constant on $M$-closures.
For flats $X\le Y$,
\[
\rho^\perp(Y)-\rho^\perp(X)
 =\rk(Y)-\rk(X)-\bigl(\rho_N(X^\perp)-\rho_N(Y^\perp)\bigr)
\]
is between $0$ and $\rk(Y)-\rk(X)$ by the strong-map increment
inequality.  Submodularity on flats follows from modularity of
$\rk_M$ and submodularity of $\rho_N$, since $^\perp$ interchanges meet
and join.  Extension by $M$-closure gives a monotone, submodular,
unit-increment rank function on all subsets.  The basis statement is
immediate from (3).  Comparing the displayed increments for
$N_p$ and $N_q$ gives the final quotient assertion.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[elementary opposition]\label{lem:elementary}
Let $p\preceq q\preceq\mu_M$, with $\rk p=d$ and $\rk q=d+1$.  Then
$q^{\perp_M}$ and $p^{\perp_M}$ satisfy all adjacent three-term
relations of ranks $r-d-1$ and $r-d$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Fix $A$ of size $r-d-2$ and distinct $x,y,z\notin A$.  Put
$U=\cl(A)$, $W=\cl(Axyz)$, and $\delta=\rk W-\rk U$.  If $A$ is
dependent or $\delta\le1$, all three terms are $\infty$.

If $\delta=2$, let $G=W^\perp$ and $H=U^\perp$.  Loops in the rank-two
interval $[U,W]$ give only $\infty$ terms, and parallel points give
equal finite terms.  Otherwise choose a basis of $G$, representatives
of the three dual points in $[G,H]$, and a common complement to an
$M$-basis.  The incidence relation for $q\preceq\mu_M$ has exactly the
three required finite $q$-terms, after adding the common $p$-coordinate.

If $\delta=3$, the three elements form a Boolean frame over $U$.  In the
dual interval, the flats
$(\cl(Ayz))^\perp$, $(\cl(Axz))^\perp$, $(\cl(Axy))^\perp$ are the three
atoms over $W^\perp$.  Choosing representatives of these atoms, the
adjacent incidence relation for $p\preceq q$ is exactly the transformed
three-term relation.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:set}
The formula $\nu\mapsto\nu^{\perp_M}$ defines an involution of the set
underlying $\Dr(M)$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Insert $\nu$ in the complete flag (1).  Lemma \ref{lem:ordinary}
reverses the ordinary supports, and Lemma \ref{lem:elementary} gives all
adjacent three-term relations for
$h_r^{\perp_M},h_{r-1}^{\perp_M},\ldots,h_0^{\perp_M}$.  The endpoints
are the trivial rank $0$ valuation and $\mu_M$.  Proposition
\ref{prop:flagcriterion} makes the reversed sequence a complete
valuated flag, so in particular $\nu^{\perp_M}\preceq\mu_M$.
Involutivity follows from (2) and $(X^\perp)^\perp=X$.
\end{proof}

\section*{3. Order reversal}

\begin{lemma}[circuit minima]\label{lem:circuit}
Let $w\preceq\mu_M$ have rank $k\ge1$, let $A$ be an independent
$(k-1)$-set of $M$, and let $C$ be a circuit of $M/A$ with $|C|\ge2$.
Then $\min_{i\in C}w(A\cup i)$ is attained at least twice.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let $F=\cl(A\cup C)$.  For every $i\in C$, $A\cup(C-i)$ is independent
and spans $F$.  Choose one complement $D$ of $F$ to an $M$-basis.  In
the incidence relation for $w\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $A$ and upper
set $A\cup C\cup D$, the finite $\mu_M$-terms are exactly those indexed
by $i\in C$.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}\label{thm:order}
If $p\preceq q\preceq\mu_M$, then
\[
        q^{\perp_M}\preceq p^{\perp_M}.
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let $\rk p=a$ and $\rk q=b$.  If $a=b$, then $p$ and $q$ are
projectively equal: the equal-rank incidence relation on adjacent bases
shows $p(B)-q(B)$ is constant on the common basis graph.  If $a=0$ or
$b=r$, the assertion is respectively $q^{\perp_M}\preceq\mu_M$ or the
vacuous rank-zero lower quotient.  Hence assume $0<a<b<r$.

By Proposition \ref{prop:set} the polars are valuated matroids, and by
Lemma \ref{lem:ordinary} their ordinary supports are in quotient order.
By Criterion \ref{crit:local} it remains to prove nested incidence.
Fix $A\subseteq T$ with
$|A|=r-b-1$, $|T|=r-a+1$, set $B=T\setminus A$, and
$m=|B|=b-a+2$.  If $A$ is dependent all terms are $\infty$.  Otherwise
put $U=\cl(A)$, $W=\cl(T)$, and $\delta=\rk W-\rk U$.  A finite term
forces $T-i$ to be independent, hence $\delta\ge m-1$; since
$\delta\le m$, only two cases remain.

If $\delta=m$, then $T$ is independent over $U$.  Let $G=W^\perp$ and
$H=U^\perp$, of ranks $a-1$ and $b+1$.  The interval generated by $B$
is Boolean; after applying $^\perp$, the flats
$X_i=(\cl(T-i))^\perp$ are the atoms over $G$, and
$Y_i=(\cl(Ai))^\perp$ is the join of all $X_j$ with $j\ne i$.  Choose a
basis $S$ of $G$ and representatives $x_i\in X_i\setminus G$.  The
nested incidence relation for $p\preceq q$ with lower set $S$ and upper
set $S\cup\{x_i:i\in B\}$ is exactly the desired transformed relation.

If $\delta=m-1$, then $B$ has nullity one in $M/A$, hence a unique
circuit $C$.  A loop circuit gives only $\infty$ terms.  Otherwise, for
$i\notin C$ the set $T-i$ is dependent; for $i\in C$ it is independent
and spans $W$, so the $p^{\perp_M}(T-i)$ factor is the common value
$\bar p(W^\perp)$.  Lemma \ref{lem:circuit}, applied to
$w=q^{\perp_M}$ and the circuit $C$ of $M/A$, gives two minima among
the remaining $q^{\perp_M}(Ai)$ terms.  This proves nested incidence.
\end{proof}

\section*{4. Agreement with flats}

Let $F$ be a flat of rank $f$, and let $q_F$ be the trivial rank
$r-f$ valuated matroid of $M/F\oplus U_{0,F}$.  For an independent
$(r-f)$-set $B$ with $Z=\cl(B)$,
\[
        q_F(B)<\infty\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Z\vee F=\hat1.
\]
For an independent $f$-set $A$ with $Y=\cl(A)$, formula (2) gives
\[
 q_F^{\perp_M}(A)<\infty
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Y^\perp\vee F=\hat1 .
\]
By modularity, since $\rk(Y^\perp)=r-f$ and $\rk F=f$, this is
equivalent to $Y^\perp\wedge F=\hat0$.  Applying the anti-automorphism
gives $Y\vee F^\perp=\hat1$, the basis condition for
$M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}$.  The finite values are zero on both
sides.  Thus the polar transform sends the flat $F$ to $F^\perp$.

Combining Proposition \ref{prop:set}, Theorem \ref{thm:order}, and this
flat computation, the map
\[
        \Phi([\nu])=[\nu^{\perp_M}]
\]
is an order-reversing involution of $\Dr(M)$ extending the given
order-reversing involution of $\cL(M)$.

"""

Two-rank endpoint criterion.  Let \(p,q\) be valuated matroids of ranks
\(a<b\), with ordinary supports in quotient order.  The nested
incidence relations
\[
\mathcal I_{a,b}(p,q;S,T)\quad(S\subseteq T,\ |S|=a-1,\ |T|=b+1)
\]
imply the full arbitrary BEZ incidence relations.  The clean proof uses
cocircuit generation, not \(M^\natural\)-convexity of the two
non-adjacent layers.  For \(I\in\binom E{a-1}\) write the valuated
cocircuit \(C_p^*(I)_e=p(Ie)\).  Fix \(J\in\binom E{b+1}\) and set
\(c_j=q(J-j)\) for \(j\in J\).  If all \(c_j=\infty\), the incidence
relation is trivial.  Otherwise \(J\) contains a \(q\)-basis \(Q\), and
the ordinary quotient rank-increment inequality gives
\[
a-\rk_p(Q)\le b-\rk_q(Q)=0,
\]
so \(J\) spans \(p\) and \(p|J\) has rank \(a\).  The nested relations
with upper set \(J\) say that \(c\) is orthogonal to every cocircuit of
\(p|J\).  By the Dress--Wenzel/Speyer cocircuit-generation theorem, the
tropical linear space of \(p|J\) is the tropical convex hull of its
cocircuits; the hyperplane of vectors orthogonal to fixed \(c\) is
tropically convex, so it contains all vectors of \(p|J\).  Finally
\(C_p^*(I)|J\) is a vector of \(p|J\), since orthogonality to each
circuit \(D\in\binom J{a+1}\) is exactly the Pluecker relation
\[
\min_{e\in D}\{p(Ie)+p(D-e)\}\ \text{twice}.
\]
Thus \(C_p^*(I)|J\perp c\), which is the full incidence relation.

Complete-flag adjacent criterion.  Jidong Wang's theorem
(arXiv:2412.12059v2, Theorem \(\ref{thm:dressian-cut-out-by-three-term}\)
in his source) states that for any valuated matroid \(\mu\), the
codimension-one relative Dressian \(\Dr^1(\mu)\) is cut out by the
three-term incidence relations.  Descending from the top layer of a
complete sequence \(w_0,\ldots,w_r\), this proves that adjacent
three-term incidence against an already-valuated \(w_{j+1}\) forces
\(w_j\) to be a valuated matroid and an elementary quotient of
\(w_{j+1}\).  Iterating gives a complete valuated flag.  This supplies
the needed nonuniform and \(\infty\)-valued replacement for the finite
uniform theorem of Joswig--Loho--Luber--Olarte.

The earlier attempted shortcut through Murota--Shioura's local
\(M^\natural\)-exchange theorem is not appropriate for the two-rank
endpoint criterion: the union of two non-adjacent basis layers need not
be an \(M^\natural\)-convex domain.  Murota's truncation/relative
elongation theorems are still used separately for the valuated Higgs
completion between \(\nu\) and \(\mu_M\).

Updated the canonical files and compile-checked `answer.tex`.

Summary of changes:
- Closed the two-rank nested-to-full incidence gap using a cocircuit-generation argument.
- Replaced the open complete-flag criterion with Wang’s codimension-one relative Dressian theorem; Wang’s arXiv record is current as v2, revised March 7, 2026. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059))
- Tightened edge cases in the order-reversal theorem: \(a=b\), \(a=0\), and \(b=r\).
- Kept BEZ as the quotient/incidence and tropical-linear-space inclusion dictionary; their Proposition 4.2.3 identifies valuated quotients with incidence-Plücker relations. ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))
- Ran `pdflatex` successfully; output is 5 pages and satisfies the stated LaTeX contract.
